chains.question: How accurately can frequency combs measure absolute optical frequencies?
chains.limit: Comb frequency ruler accuracy limited by f_CEO and f_rep traceability
chains.best: Sub-10^-16 absolute uncertainty; 100-200 as residual timing; <10^-20 instability at 10^4 s

chains.question: What limits the phase noise of optical frequency comb teeth, and how can spectral purity be transferred from optical reference to microwave?
chains.limit: Gain lifetime limits f_CEO servo bandwidth
chains.best: f_CEO integrated phase noise ~50 mrad; optical-to-microwave transfer at 10⁻¹⁸ level
chains.narrative:
The optical frequency comb's defining capability — transferring spectral purity from an optical reference to microwave frequencies — is fundamentally limited by the phase noise on each comb tooth. This noise originates from three coupled sources.
First, the servo bandwidth for stabilizing f_CEO is limited by the Er:fiber gain lifetime (~10 ms), restricting pump-modulation bandwidth to ~20 kHz. Kus

chains.question: What limits the phase noise and timing jitter of optical frequency combs, and how close are we to the quantum limit?
chains.limit: Gain lifetime limits f_CEO servo bandwidth
chains.best: 10 GHz absolute timing jitter: 420 attoseconds (1 Hz-5 GHz)

chains.question: What determines the spectral bandwidth of frequency combs, and how has octave-spanning coverage been achieved?

chains.question: What limits the instability of optical frequency transfer over fiber links, and what is the path forward?
chains.limit: Two-way noise rejection via bidirectional transmission
chains.best: σ_y = 5×10⁻²¹ @ 4,000 s (100 km); (0.5±2.0)×10⁻²¹ residual over 33 days
chains.narrative:
Optical frequency transfer over fiber faced two fundamental limits: compensation non-reciprocity and polarization mode dispersion (PMD).
Non-reciprocity has been SOLVED. Calosso (2013) demonstrated that two-way passive phase comparison eliminates the PLL bandwidth constraint. Bercy (2014) reached the fundamental non-reciprocity floor at 5×10⁻²¹ @ 4,000 s. Xu (2019) confirmed that residual non-reci

chains.question: What determines the ultimate frequency stability of fiber delay-line (FDL) stabilized lasers, and how can it be improved?
chains.best: frequency noise < 0.8 Hz²/Hz @ 1 Hz; σ_y ≈ 7×10⁻¹⁵ @ 0.1-1 s
chains.narrative:
Fiber delay-line (FDL) stabilization offers a fundamentally different path from FP cavities: compact, tunable, vacuum-free, and SWaP-optimized for field deployment. The cost is ~100× worse ultimate stability.
The story begins in 2009 when Kéfélian demonstrated the first all-fiber delay-line lock achieving 8 Hz linewidth — competitive with early FP cavities. Jiang (2010) extended this to agile swep

chains.question: What limits optical time-frequency transfer through free space, and how close are we to satellite-based global clock networks?
chains.best: sigma_y = 6.1e-21 @ 300 s (2 km); <4e-19 @ 113 km; 102 dB link budget supports ground-GEO

chains.question: What are the fundamental limits to optical atomic clock stability and systematic uncertainty, and how are they being overcome?

edges.relation: BOUNDED-BY (practical, dominates when laser noise > QPN)

chains.narrative:
Optical atomic clocks face two fundamental stability limits that together determine the ultimate clock performance.
Quantum projection noise (QPN) is the irreducible quantum limit. For single-ion clocks (²⁷Al⁺, ¹⁷¹Yb⁺), overcoming QPN requires long interrogation times (T→1 s) and ultrastable clock lasers (cross-topic: ultrastable-laser chain). For lattice clocks (⁸⁷Sr, ¹⁷¹Yb), the large atom numbe

chains.question: What are the scientific and metrological requirements for redefining the SI second from microwave (Cs) to optical frequency standards?
chains.limit: CCTF criteria for SI second redefinition
chains.best: Roadmap target: ≥3 independent optical clocks with systematic uncertainty < 10⁻¹⁸ and ≥3 independent frequency ratio measurements at <5×10⁻¹⁸
chains.narrative:
The SI second has been defined by the Cs hyperfine transition (9.192631770 GHz) since 1967. Optical clocks now outperform Cs fountains by 100× in accuracy (<10⁻¹⁸ vs 3×10⁻¹⁶), motivating redefinition based on an optical transition.
The CCTF roadmap (Dimarcq 2024) requires: validated clocks, independent ratio measurements, and demonstrated reliability. The scientific motivation extends beyond metro

chains.question: What determines the ultimate fractional frequency instability σ_y(τ≈1s) of FP cavity-stabilized lasers, and how can it be improved?

edges.to: Next: GaP/SiN coatings, φ < 10⁻⁶ at cryo

chains.narrative:
The FP cavity σ_y limit is a story of three parallel approaches converging on a single goal.
In 1999, Young demonstrated sub-Hz linewidths but was limited by ULE substrate thermal noise at ~3×10⁻¹⁶. Numata (2004) formalized the FDT theory, showing that mirror coatings dominate the thermal noise budget for typical cavities.
Three breakthroughs followed:
1. COATING PATH: Cole (2013) demonstrated AlG

consensus.name: Allan deviation noise type identification

timeline.system: Allan — Statistics of Atomic Frequency Standards
timeline.significance: Solved classical variance divergence for flicker noise; became universal standard
Established systematic methodology

timeline.system: Allan and Barnes — phase averaging kernel
timeline.significance: Distinguishes WPM from FPM
All variance measures and relationships
Practical handbook for clock metrologists
Complete reference with confidence intervals and software
Fundamentals for modern optical clock metrology

consensus.trend: Allan deviation remains the universal diagnostic. The mathematical foundation established in 1966-2008 is complete; current work focuses on extending to optical clock regimes and inter-comparison methodologies.

consensus.name: Optical frequency comb absolute timing jitter and phase noise

timeline.system: McFerran — first Er:fiber comb f_CEO lock
timeline.significance: First characterization of f_CEO phase noise; 80 kHz servo bandwidth

timeline.system: Le Coq — CSO-referenced optical frequency division
timeline.significance: First CSO → comb → microwave chain with record phase noise
First sub-femtosecond absolute timing jitter
Near fundamental thermal limits; benchmark for a decade
Lowest demonstrated microwave phase noise from OFD
Bypasses EDFA gain lifetime servo bandwidth limit
Sub-radian integrated phase noise for both degrees of freedom

timeline.system: Endo — BOMPD locked to ultrastable laser
timeline.significance: Sub-fs residual timing jitter between comb modes

timeline.value: 0.12 fs f_beat jitter; 0.55 fs f_CEO jitter
timeline.significance: Record 120 attosecond f_beat timing jitter
Lowest demonstrated 8 GHz phase noise floor

timeline.value: 139 as f_beat jitter; 168 mrad f_beat phase noise
timeline.system: Wang — digital servo all-PM Er:fiber comb
timeline.significance: Current record: 139 attosecond absolute timing jitter

consensus.trend: Timing jitter improved from 760 as (2011) → 420 as (2012) → 139 as (2025) — a 5.5× improvement over 14 years. The rate of improvement slowed after 2012 as systems approached fundamental thermal and shot noise limits.

consensus.name: Optical frequency transfer instability — by distance scale

timeline.significance: First demonstration; active PLL compensation
National fiber network proven for clock comparison
Non-reciprocity compensation proven: passive scheme eliminates PLL bandwidth limit
Passive scheme reaches fundamental non-reciprocity floor
Non-reciprocity fundamentally confirmed at 10⁻²¹ level — this limit is SOLVED

timeline.system: Clivati — PMD mitigation via polarization diversity
timeline.significance: PMD identified as the NEXT dominant limit; dual-pol receiver demonstrated
Free-space approaching fiber parity at metro scale
Ground↔GEO satellite demonstrated feasible

consensus.trend: Non-reciprocity compensation is SOLVED (Xu 2019). The current bottleneck is PMD — polarization fading causes detection loss-of-lock, limiting operational duty cycle. Dual-polarization receivers (Clivati 2020) are the key breakthrough. Next frontier: PMD compensation for >500 km links and space-based global clock networks.

consensus.name: Free-space optical time-frequency transfer instability

timeline.value: Tip-tilt active tracking demonstrated at 12 km
timeline.system: Sinclair — 12 km free-space optical clock comparison

timeline.value: Atmospheric timing jitter PSD: S_jitter ∝ f^(-6.7/3)

consensus.trend: Free-space transfer is now a Nature-published field. Shen (2022) set the 113 km benchmark. Caldwell (2023) proved GEO is feasible. The next Nature paper in this sequence should demonstrate LEO satellite transfer.

consensus.name: Optical atomic clock systematic uncertainty and instability

timeline.significance: Cold-atom fountain established as microwave standard
Cs fountain approaching 10⁻¹⁶ accuracy
Optical clock instability surpassing Cs fountains by 1000×

timeline.system: Bloom — JILA Sr optical lattice clock
timeline.significance: First optical clock with <10⁻¹⁷ systematic uncertainty
Three species at <10⁻¹⁷; SI redefinition criteria met
Dick effect eliminated; instability reaches 10⁻¹⁸
Clock-based geodetic measurement at cm level
Sr lattice clock breaks 10⁻¹⁸ barrier

timeline.system: Fortier review — state of the field
timeline.significance: Al⁺ quantum logic clock holds record; lattice clocks at ~5×10⁻¹⁷/√τ instability

consensus.trend: Systematic uncertainty improved 1000× from Cs fountain (10⁻¹⁶, 1995) to Al⁺ logic clock (5.5×10⁻¹⁹, 2026). Multiple species now at <10⁻¹⁸. The 10⁻¹⁹ barrier is being breached. SI second redefinition is technically satisfied.

consensus.name: SI second realization stability and redefinition roadmap
consensus.best: 5.5×10⁻¹⁹ (²⁷Al⁺ NIST) — optical clocks surpass Cs fountain by >1000×

timeline.value: SI second defined by ¹³³Cs hyperfine transition
timeline.significance: Foundation of atomic timekeeping
Earth rotation vs atomic time divergence; motivation for redefinition debate

timeline.system: Clairon — first Cs fountain at BNM-SYRTE
timeline.significance: Cold-atom fountain surpasses thermal beam standards
Three-fountain ensemble; 48% of global TAI calibration
Highest resolution Cs fountain comparison for TAI
Multiple optical clock species at <10⁻¹⁷; CCTF redefinition criteria met
Global participation in optical clock development for redefinition

consensus.trend: Cs fountain accuracy plateaued at ~10⁻¹⁶ by 2005. Optical clocks reached 10⁻¹⁸ by 2014 and continue improving toward 10⁻¹⁹. The CCTF roadmap is technically satisfied; the remaining barrier is operational reliability and international consensus, not physics.

timeline.significance: Foundation technology

timeline.system: Young 1999 — ULE cavity, 563 nm dye laser
timeline.significance: First sub-Hz linewidth; established ULE reference cavity platform

timeline.system: Numata 2004 — FDT thermal noise theory for FP cavities
timeline.significance: Established coating thermal noise as the ultimate limit

timeline.system: Webster 2008 — First comprehensive thermal noise verification
timeline.significance: Confirmed that observed noise floor matches FDT prediction
Si cryogenic platform: 1000× higher Q than ULE, sub-40 mHz linewidth
10× lower coating mechanical loss than IBS; opened path to 10⁻¹⁷
Demonstrated that long cavities can reach 10⁻¹⁷ territory without cryogenics

timeline.system: Matei 2017 — 124 K Si cavity + IBS coating
timeline.significance: Proved that cryogenic Si reaches coating-limited thermal noise floor
First rigorous 4K characterization; discovered power-dependent drift

timeline.system: Parke 2025 — 68 cm cavity, 295 μs storage time
timeline.significance: Longest demonstrated cavity; active RAM cancellation to 10⁻¹⁹ level

timeline.system: Chen 2025 — 10 cm Si cavity at sub-5 K
timeline.significance: Sub-5K operation; still IBS-limited, points to AlGaAs as next step

timeline.system: Lee 2026 — 17 K Si + AlGaAs crystalline coating
timeline.significance: Convergence of cryogenic Si + crystalline coating paths; four times better than IBS
